1.description: Current income, capital gains, and net withdrawals. The figure shows a scatter plot of monthly net withdrawals against current income yields (Panel A) or capital gains (Panel B) in the same month. Following Baker, Nagel, and Wurgler (2007), withdrawals are defined as households' monthly net withdrawals from their brokerage account scaled by the account value in the previous month. Income yields are defined as the sum of stock dividends and bond coupons scaled by the account value in the previous month. Capital gains are defined as the total price changes scaled by the account value in the previous month. The graph is truncated at 4% for both axes to drop outliers. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Dividend yields, deposit rates, and fed funds rate. This figure shows the aggregate U.S. stock market dividend yield, the interest rates of three-month CDs, and the fed funds rate from 1963 to 2016. The aggregate stock market dividend yield is retrieved from Robert Shiller's website. The yield of three-month CDs and the fed funds rate are retrieved from the FRED database of the St. Louis Fed. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Fund flows to money market funds and bank CDs. The solid lines plot the time series of annual fund flows for money market funds (Panel A) and bank CDs (Panel B); the dashed lines are the changes in the fed funds rate over the past three years. The data are at a quarterly frequency. The data source is the FRED database of the St. Louis Fed. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Impulse response of fund flows to changes in the fed funds rate. The solid and dashed lines in each figure plot, respectively, the impulse responses of the high- and low-income mutual fund flows to a negative 1% shock to the fed funds rate; the thin dotted lines represent 95% confidence intervals. The standard errors are clustered by month. The estimation model is given by equation (8). The estimation sample compares domestic mutual funds in the United States from 1991 to 2016. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Changes in the dividend premium and the fed funds rate. The figure shows a scatter plot of the annual change in the dividend premium against the contemporaneous annual change in the fed funds rate. We take equal-weighted averages of the market-to-book ratio separately for dividend payers and nonpayers in each year and compute the dividend premium as the difference in the two average log market-to-book ratios (Baker and Wurgler (2004b)). The sample period is from 1963 to 2016. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Cumulative return of the dividend strategy. The figure plots the cumulative risk-adjusted return of the dividend strategy that  buys the 10th decile of the dividend portfolio and shorts the first decile after a negative one-year change in the fed funds rate, and buys the first decile of the dividend portfolio and shorts the 10th decile after a positive one-year change in the fed funds rate. Dividend strategy (lagn) is defined as the same long-short portfolios as the baseline, but the portfolio adjustment is made with an-year lag. Retail investor returns are calculated using a strategy that mimics the flows to high-income equity funds when the fed funds rate changes. The returns are normalized to have the same monthly standard deviation of 1%. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Impulse response of excess returns of dividend-sorted portfolios. The solid lines in each figure plot the impulse response (βi,hfrom regression (10)) of the Fama-French five-factor alphas of the 10 dividend decile portfolios to a negative 1% shock to the fed funds rate. The dotted lines represent 95% confidence intervals. The sample period is from July 1963 to June 2016. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: Impulse response of excess returns of dividend strips. The solid lines in each figure plot the impulse response (βi,hfrom regression (11)) of the Fama-French five-factor alphas of the dividend strips to a negative 1% shock to the fed funds rate. Panel A has a horizon of five years with an annual frequency. Panel B has a horizon of one year with a monthly frequency. The dotted lines represent 95% confidence intervals. The sample period is from June 1989 to October 2009. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

9.description: Monetary policy and consumption. The figure plots the equilibrium expected consumption at time 1 of AgentA. Expected consumption is computed as the probability weighted average of consumption in the four possible states. The upper and lower lines plot consumption in the unconstrained and constrained equilibrium, respectively. Preferences parameter values are:γA=γB=3,δA=δB=0.98. We assume that the dividend growth of both endowment trees has volatilityσH=σL=0.2and correlationρ=0.5. AssetH(value stock) has expected dividend growth rateμH=0.02and assetL(growth stock) has expected dividend growth rateμL=0.04. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

10.description: Monetary policy and portfolio holdings. The figure plots the holdings of the High- and Low-dividend assets as a function of the nominal interest rate, for the constrained and unconstrained portfolios. For illustrative purposes, we take asset returns as given. The portfolio(θH,θL)refers, respectively, to the holdings of the high- and low-dividend-paying asset. The realized return to each asset in each period has a binomial distribution with realizationsRuj=eμj−12σj2+σjandRdj=eμj−12σj2−σj,j=L,H, whereμH=μL=0.07andσH=σL=0.2. The probability distribution of outcomes isPr(RuH,RuL)=Pr(RdH,RdL)=(1+ρ)/4andPr(RuH,RdL)=Pr(RdH,RuL)=(1−ρ)/4, withρ=0.5. To implement the income constraint (20), we assume that the dividend yielddpH=0.8anddpL=0.01. The agent has risk-aversion parameterγ=3and time preference parameterδ=0.98. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

11.description: Monetary policy and the dividend premium. The figure plots the dividend premium as a function of the nominal risk-free rates in the general equilibrium model of two agents. Preferences parameter values:γA=γB=3,δA=δB=0.98. We assume that the dividend growth of both endowment trees has volatilityσH=σL=0.2and correlationρ=0.5. AssetH(value stock) has expected dividend growth rateμH=0.02and assetL(growth stock) has expected dividend growth rateμL=0.04. Initial aggregate endowment:ωA=ωB=0.5. The dividend premium is the ratio of the risk premium of the growth stock and the value stock minus one. We normalize the dividend premium for the unconstrained economy to zero to facilitate comparison. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

12.description: Monetary policy and portfolio volatility. The figure plots the volatility of the time-0 portfolio as a function of the nominal interest rate. For illustrative purposes, we take asset returns as given. The realized return to each asset in each period has a binomial distribution with realizationsRuj=eμj−12σj2+σjandRdj=eμj−12σj2−σj,j=L,H, whereμH=μL=0.07andσH=σL=0.2. The probability distribution of outcomes isPr(RuH,RuL)=Pr(RdH,RdL)=(1+ρ)/4andPr(RuH,RdL)=Pr(RdH,RuL)=(1−ρ)/4, withρ=0.5. To implement the income constraint (20), we assume that the dividend yielddpH=0.8anddpL=0.01. The agent has risk-aversion parameterγ=3and time preference parameterδ=0.98. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

13.description: Dividend initiations and the fed funds rate. The figure reports the time series plot of the fed funds rate and the frequency of dividend and repurchase initiations in next year scaled by the total number of firms in the Compustat database. The sample includes all Compustat firms from 1962 to 2016. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
